query: GRG5 gene function in mouse embryonic development

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Bowel -- Cellular
retrieved_docs.content: Lgr5 + stem cells (leucine-rich repeat-containing G-protein coupled receptor 5) develop during the growth of intestinal villi and crypts and are essential for the biochemical balance of the intestine. These stem cells are also present in the formed intestine.

retrieved_docs.title: Lissencephaly -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The ARX gene encodes for the aristaless related homeobox protein, which is a transcription factor with an important role in the forebrain and other tissue. [14] Children with ARX mutation present with other symptoms such as the absence of portions of the brain (corpus callosum agenesis), abnormal genitalia, and severe epilepsy. [15] [16] RELN RELN gene encodes reelin, an extracellular matrix glycoprotein. The mutation of this gene is involved in lissencephaly associated with cerebellar hypoplasia and hippocampal abnormalities. [17] Other genes: Several genes have established a relationship with lissencephaly. Those genes are VLDLR, ACTB, ACTG1, TUBG1, KIF5C, KIF2A, and CDK5. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Heart -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The establishment of laterality is an essential aspect of cardiac development, facilitated by cilia configuration within the primitive node. Ciliary structures found in the primitive node have a unique design that allows for overall leftward migration with the failure of such a process, leading to dextrocardia conditions. The motile cilia rotate in a clockwise fashion to generate this movement. The determination of the left-right cardiac axis is via several genes typically induced by NODAL expression. In turn, the NODAL expression induces expression of left-right determination factor ( LEFTY ) gene expression and paired-like homeodomain transcription factor 2 ( PITX2 ). [6] The PITX2 gene is considered the primary gene responsible for the determination of left-right orientation. The PITX2 gene has several roles in cardiac development. In addition to establishing left-right orientation, PITX2 gene mutations have associations with isolated cardiac congenital heart disease, including atrial septal defect, ventricular septal defect, and tetralogy of Fallot. [7] Even aside from mutations, some studies have emphasized the global effects of regulatory gene knockout on embryonic lethalities, such as the demonstrated importance of VangI2 on cardiac outflow tract development and Noggin on contractility. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Gastrulation -- Biochemical
retrieved_docs.content: RNA helicase A (RHA) can function as a helicase for both RNA and DNA. The sequence and biochemical conservation of RHA and its homologs to humans suggests an evolutionarily conserved function. Normal gastrulation depends on RNA helicase A activity, as lack of proper RHA signaling results in ectodermal cell death with clear alterations in differentiation. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Gastrulation -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Another important function of gastrulation is to establish directionality within the developing embryo. Cranial/caudal directionality is established by the placement of the prechordal plate and the path of the primitive groove. The layering of the epiblast and hypoblast establishes the dorsal/ventral axis.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Neural Tube -- Molecular Level -- Cilia
retrieved_docs.content: These are hair-like structures that help to regulate signal transduction with the use of mechanical molecular cues. Cilia are essential in major signaling pathways such as the sonic hedgehog pathway during neural tube formation. Approximately 5% of neural tube disorders have a relation to genes that are responsible for cilia formation and function. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Gastrulation -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Gastrulation involves a complex series of cellular morphogenesis, cellular movements, and cell signaling via transcription factors, chemical morphogenic gradients, and differential gene expression to allow for the induction of germ cell layer formation that orchestrates the initiation of eventual organ system development.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tyrosine Kinase Receptors -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Signaling pathways triggered by growth factor-RTK binding play a pivotal role in embryonic and fetal development. This knowledge comes from studies in which RTK genes are intentionally knocked out or altered, as well as the study of clinical disorders of organogenesis. Almost all RTKs and their growth factors function in some capacity in organ formation; notable examples appear below.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Neural Tube -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The molecular arrangement for the edges of the neural plate to meet and fuse have multiple genes and proteins that facilitate proper apposition. Major ones are in this section:

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tyrosine Kinase Receptors -- Development -- ErbB/Epidermal Growth Factor
retrieved_docs.content: Epidermal growth factor (EGFR) and neuregulins (ie, ErbB1) receptors are present diffusely on embryonic and fetal tissue; mice lacking EGFR die in utero or the early postnatal period, and have defects in numerous tissues including skin, central nervous system, intestines, liver, and lung. ErbB2 and ErbB4 knock-out mice fail to form cardiac ventricle trabeculae, leading to intrauterine death. ErbB4 knock-outs also have defects in cranial sensory ganglia and peripheral nervous system myelination due to defects in Schwann cell migration. ErbB3 knock-out mice have impaired atrioventricular valve formation with early embryonic demise. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Central Nervous System, Malformations -- Clinical Significance -- Lissencephaly-Pachygyria
retrieved_docs.content: Lissencephaly-pachygyria is a spectrum of abnormal development of the cerebral gyri and sulci resulting from the abnormal migration of neurons. The term for a partial development is pachygyria, and complete absence is agyria. Pachygyria typically have milder symptoms compared to lissencephaly. Type one is known to be the classic lissencephaly, and type two is the cobblestone complex. [30] [31] The cobblestone lissencephaly malformation is associated with the TUBA1A and GPR56 gene mutations. The cobblestone defect results from the combination of the excessive migration of neural crest cells into the leptomeninges and abnormalities in the surface of the cerebral pia layer. The commonest gene mutations implicated in lissencephaly are LIS1 and DCX. Others include cell structure proteins like  actin, dynein, kinesin, tubulin genes, CASP2, and RIPK1 domain-containing adaptor with death domain (CRADD). [32] [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Gastrulation -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Gastrulation occurs during week 3 of human development. The process of gastrulation generates the three primary germ layers ectoderm, endoderm, and mesoderm. Gastrulation primes the system for organogenesis and is one of the most critical steps of development.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Hand -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: At the molecular level, protein factors, such as ligands, receptors and transcription factors, play a critical role in the development of the limb. Sonic hedgehog (Shh) and fibroblast growth factors are specific ligands and serve important roles in cell signaling. These ligand factors induce differentiation and proliferation of neighboring cell populations within the nascent limb. These ligands stimulate signal transduction to cause structural and functional changes in cell behavior. This causes transcription factors to travel into the nucleus of the cell and create new DNA. [5] Both of these factors lead to the expression of new genes. Research continues to clarify the partially understood mechanisms of the molecular biology of the developing hand.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Genitourinary -- Development -- Genital System
retrieved_docs.content: The initial appearance of the gonads is the genital (gonadal) ridges formed by the proliferation of the epithelium and condensation of underlying mesenchyme. The development of testes in males and ovaries in females relies on the induction by primordial germ cells from the yolk sac to the genital (gonadal) ridges during the 4th through 6th weeks. These primordial cells arrive at the primitive gonads at week 5 and invade the gonadal ridge during week 6. [6] The presence of the SRY gene ( s ex-determining r egion on the Y chromosome) on the short arm of the Y chromosome (Yp11) influences their development into testes, while the gonads will become ovaries in the absence of the gene. The presence of estrogen is paramount in the formation of external genitalia in females, whereas testosterone drives the development of male external genitalia.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Branchial Arches -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The molecular basis underlying the formation of the branchial arch derivatives is dependent on a vast array of genes that facilitate proper growth. This section will highlight many of the important genes identified in this process.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Branchial Arches -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Dlx genes help to establish the pattern and polarity of both neural crest cell-derived facial bones and the first branchial arch. Similar to the relationship of RA to Hox genes, endothelin signaling serves to regulate Dlx gene expression as an upstream regulator. Studies involving various Dlx gene deletions in mice suggest that regulation of the formation of the lower jaw is by Dlx transcription factor activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 675
retrieved_docs.content: Synthetic estrogens, plain are classified as estrogens, which are sex hormones and modulators of the genital system. As a synthetic form of these hormones, they function within the same physiological pathways as natural estrogens, influencing reproductive tissue development and function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2695
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnen (4) derivatives, progestogens are classified as members of the progesterone family, indicating that they share structural and functional characteristics with the hormone progesterone.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnen (4) derivatives, progestogens are classified as progestogen sex hormones and modulators of the genital system, and they are considered members of the progesterone family. As such, they function as hormonal agents that influence reproductive physiology and genital system regulation, sharing the core structural and functional characteristics of progesterone.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2773
retrieved_docs.content: Erythropetin and glukagon are both members of the broad class “Amino Acid, Peptide, or Protein.” As peptide hormones, they share the structural characteristic of being protein‑based signaling molecules, yet they serve very different physiological functions. Erythropetin regulates erythropoiesis by stimulating the production of red blood cells, while glukagon raises blood glucose levels by promoting glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis. Recognizing that these distinct hormones belong to the same molecular category highlights their common biochemical nature and informs both basic understanding of hormone biology and the development of protein‑based therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 345
retrieved_docs.content: trisaccharides qualify biosynthetic processes, particularly in the synthesis of gonadotropin glycoproteins; choriogonadotropin and follitropin β belong to the gonadotropin structural class and incorporate specific trisaccharide moieties that affect hormone stability, receptor interaction, and biological activity. Understanding these hierarchical drug classifications, their biosynthetic origins, and the diverse molecular modifiers that regulate biosynthesis aids clinicians in selecting appropriate immunosuppressive therapy and in appreciating the complex mechanisms underlying hormone production and other therapeutic contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2955
retrieved_docs.content: Luteinizing Hormone‑releasing Hormone (LHRH) agonists constitute a class of compounds that stimulate the gonadotropin‑releasing hormone receptor to modulate pituitary hormone release, and triptorelin is a specific agent that belongs to this LHRH‑agonist class. Recognizing triptorelin as a member of the LHRH‑agonist group is essential for understanding its mechanism of action and therapeutic applications in hormone‑dependent conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1644
retrieved_docs.content: Follitropin beta is classified within the structural class of gonadotropiny. Gonadotropiny represent a group of reproductive hormones that encompass the lidský choriogonadotropin (human chorionic gonadotropin). This classification indicates that follitropin beta shares key structural characteristics with the human chorionic gonadotropin family, underscoring its role in the regulation of gonadal function and its relevance in therapeutic applications that target the reproductive endocrine system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1418
retrieved_docs.content: PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES are classified as hypothalamic hormones, encompassing both the natural hormones secreted by the pituitary and hypothalamus and their synthetic analogues. This categorization places them within the broader group of hypothalamic hormones, highlighting their shared origin and functional relevance in endocrine regulation and therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1464
retrieved_docs.content: Choriogonadotropin and follitropin beta are both classified within the gonadotropiny structural class, indicating that they share a common protein architecture characteristic of gonadotropin hormones. As members of this structural class, both agents exhibit similar molecular features that enable them to interact with gonadal receptors and influence reproductive processes. Recognizing that choriogonadotropin and follitropin beta belong to the same gonadotropin family helps clinicians understand their comparable mechanisms of action, potential cross‑reactivity, and appropriate therapeutic uses in assisted reproduction and related endocrine treatments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1991
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism of action for merkaptamin is currently described as an unknown cellular or molecular interaction, indicating that the precise cellular or molecular processes by which merkaptamin exerts its effects have not yet been elucidated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 567
retrieved_docs.content: Gonadotropins and other ovulation stimulants are classified as a subset of sex hormones and modulators of the genital system, indicating that these agents belong to the broader category of hormonal substances that regulate and modulate genital function and reproductive processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2045
retrieved_docs.content: action is mediated by electrolyte modulation. Its broader relevance is highlighted by its fundamental presence in natural structures such as perleť, illustrating the compound’s versatility across pharmacological and biological domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2796
retrieved_docs.content: Hormony is a hormone that has the progestiny class as its parent, indicating that it is derived from or belongs to the progestin family of steroid hormones. Megestrolacetát is identified as a progestiny, meaning it is a member of the progestin class of synthetic progesterone analogs. Together, these relationships emphasize that both hormony and megestrolacetát are linked to the progestiny (progestin) lineage, reflecting their shared pharmacological and biochemical heritage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2736
retrieved_docs.content: Choriogonadotropin is classified within the structural class of gonadotropiny, indicating that it belongs to the gonadotropin family of hormones.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2768
retrieved_docs.content: Gonadotropiny is classified as a type of hormony, indicating that it belongs to the broader family of hormones. Choriogonadotropin is a specific molecule that falls within the structural class of gonadotropiny, meaning it shares the characteristic features of gonadotropins. This relationship highlights that choriogonadotropin is a hormone of the gonadotropin class, which is a subset of the larger hormony category, underscoring its role in endocrine and reproductive physiology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2730
retrieved_docs.content: ADRENAL AND GONADAL HORMONES is a category that includes the hormone deoxykortikosteron as one of its members. Deoxykortikosteron is biochemically linked to tetrahydrodeoxycorticosterone, being mapped from this precursor compound. This connection indicates that deoxykortikosteron originates from tetrahydrodeoxycorticosterone within the steroidogenesis pathway of adrenal and gonadal hormone production.
